title: Safety and Health in PT
narrationScript: Penetrant materials are engineered to be relatively low hazard, but a shop earns that with controls. This lesson covers the three hazard families a Level II manages every shift. Toxicity first. Skin contact is the everyday route — penetrant oils and solvent removers strip the skin's natural oils and cause dermatitis, so wear the gloves the safety data sheet specifies and wash with soap and water, never with solvent. Control vapors, spray mists, and developer dust with ventilation, and treat tank and confined-space work as permit territory. Flammability turns on flash point. Qualified penetrants must flash above two hundred degrees Fahrenheit, so the open dip tank is rarely the fire risk — the aerosol solvent removers and nonaqueous developers are. Store them away from heat, below about one hundred twenty degrees. Ultraviolet lamps demand two habits: never operate one with a cracked or missing filter, and never look into the beam. And the safety data sheet — sixteen standard sections under OSHA's Hazard Communication standard — is where every control begins.

sections.heading: Low Hazard Is Earned, Not Granted
sections.body:
A penetrant line looks tame next to a radiography bay — and that is exactly the danger. The materials are formulated to be relatively benign, you handle them every shift, and familiarity breeds shortcuts. PT's hazards are mostly quiet and cumulative: dermatitis that builds over weeks of bare-handed wiping, solvent vapor in a poorly ventilated corner, an aerosol can cooking on top of a dryer. Rarely dramatic, always real.

As a Level II you do more than protect yourself. You set up the line, you run the written procedure, and you direct trainees and Level I personnel — so the controls are yours to enforce. Treat safety exactly like any other process control: specified, checked, documented.

Chemicals reach the body by four routes — **skin contact, eye contact, inhalation, and ingestion** — and every control in this lesson closes one of those doors. We will work through the PT shop's three hazard families in turn — **toxicity, flammability, and ultraviolet radiation** — and finish with the document that ties them all together, the **safety data sheet (SDS)**. Throughout, your employer's written safety program and the manufacturer's SDS govern; what follows is the standard playbook they are built from.

sections.heading: Toxicity: Four Routes In, Four Sets of Controls
sections.body:
**Skin contact** is the everyday exposure. Penetrant oils, emulsifiers, and solvent removers are all excellent at dissolving grease — including the natural oils in your skin. Repeated bare-handed contact defats the skin and leads to irritation, cracking, and dermatitis, and some individuals sensitize so that ever-smaller exposures trigger a reaction. The controls are unglamorous and effective: the gloves named in SDS Section 8 (commonly nitrile for penetrant oils and many solvents), aprons for tank work, prompt soap-and-water washing, and dry cuffs — a penetrant-soaked sleeve is an all-shift exposure.

**Inhalation** matters wherever material gets into the air: solvent vapor at cleaning stations, penetrant and emulsifier mist around spray application, dry developer dust at the developer chamber. Local exhaust ventilation and spray booths are the primary controls; respirators are used where the employer's respiratory-protection program requires them. Two situations deserve special respect. Inside tanks, vessels, and other **confined spaces**, solvent vapors accumulate and can displace breathable air — that is permit-and-program territory, never improvisation. And the vapors of **chlorinated solvents**, still found in some degreasing operations, are more toxic than the petroleum solvents on the PT line and can decompose into far more dangerous gases around welding arcs and open flame.

**Ingestion and eye contact** round out the routes: no food, drink, or smoking at the process line; wash before eating; wear splash protection where the procedure requires it; and know where the eyewash station is before you need it.

sections.title: Solvent is not soap
sections.body: Cleaning penetrant off your hands or forearms with solvent remover is the classic PT bad habit. The solvent takes the dye off by taking your skin's protective oils with it — which is precisely how dermatitis starts. Wash with soap and water, wear the gloves the SDS calls for so dye never reaches skin in the first place, and save the solvent for the part.

sections.heading: Flammability: Flash Point Sets the Rules
sections.body:
**Flash point** is the number that organizes everything: the lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with the air at its surface. Below its flash point a liquid does not produce an ignitable vapor blanket; at or above it, one spark is enough. NFPA 30 turns flash point into storage classes — **Class I flammable liquids** flash below 100 °F, and Classes II, IIIA, and IIIB **combustible liquids** flash progressively higher, with Class IIIB at or above 200 °F.

Qualified penetrants sit at the safe end of the ladder: AMS 2644 requires qualified penetrants to have flash points no lower than 93 °C (200 °F), so an open dip tank at shop temperature sits far below the point where it gives off ignitable vapor. The shop's real fire risk is in the cans: **solvent removers and the carriers in nonaqueous developers** are commonly Class I materials whose vapors can ignite at room temperature.

The working rules follow directly. Keep only the working supply of aerosols and solvent at the station and store the rest in an approved flammable-liquids cabinet. Control ignition sources — no smoking, sparks, or open flame, and never spray cleaner or developer near hot surfaces such as dryer ovens or a hot mercury-lamp housing. Aerosol cans add a pressure hazard of their own: never puncture or incinerate them, and never store them above the label limit, typically 120 °F (49 °C). Ventilation does double duty here — it keeps vapor concentrations below harmful levels and below ignitable ones.

sections.caption: NFPA 30 turns closed-cup flash point into liquid classes. AMS 2644-qualified penetrants flash at or above 200 °F, while the aerosol solvents most lines depend on flash below room temperature — that is where the fire risk lives.

sections.heading: Ultraviolet Radiation: Respect the Lamp
sections.body:
Fluorescent inspection runs on **UV-A** — the 320 to 400 nm band, with lamps centered near 365 nm. UV-A is the least energetic ultraviolet band, but the lamp on your bench deserves respect for two reasons: the source behind the filter, and the intensity in the beam.

A **mercury-vapor lamp** generates raw output across the ultraviolet spectrum, including UV-B and UV-C — the wavelengths that cause photokeratitis (the painful 'welder's flash') and skin burns. The deep-violet **filter** is what turns that raw output into a safe inspection beam: it passes 320–400 nm and blocks the shorter wavelengths along with most visible light. That is why the rule is absolute: **a lamp with a cracked, loose, or missing filter comes out of service immediately.** Mercury lamps also run hot and contain mercury — let them cool before handling, and treat a broken bulb as a chemical spill, not floor sweepings. LED UV-A lamps emit a narrow band near 365 nm and, for aerospace work, are qualified per ASTM E3022; they remove the broadband-source problem but not the intensity problem.

Intensity is the second reason for respect. You must deliver at least 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A to the part surface, and the industrial-hygiene guideline for prolonged near-UV exposure of the unprotected eye — the ACGIH TLV — is of the same order, about 1 mW/cm². A beam bright enough to work with is bright enough to matter. So: never look into the lamp, point it at the part and not at people, keep hands and forearms out of the close beam during long inspections, and wear **UV-absorbing spectacles**. They block the UV-A that would otherwise make the eye's own lens fluoresce into a veiling haze — and indications get easier to see at the same time.

sections.caption: The filter, not the bulb, makes a mercury-vapor UV lamp safe: it passes the 320–400 nm UV-A band and blocks UV-B, UV-C, and most visible light. A cracked or missing filter means the lamp comes out of service.

sections.heading: The SDS: HazCom in Practice
sections.body:
Every control in this lesson traces back to one document. OSHA's **Hazard Communication standard, 29 CFR 1910.1200**, requires the manufacturer or importer of each hazardous chemical to provide a **safety data sheet**, and requires your employer to keep one for every hazardous chemical in the workplace, **readily accessible during each work shift** — a binder at the line or a terminal that is always available, not a locked office. The same standard puts GHS labels on every container — signal word, pictograms, hazard statements — and requires training so you can read both. Since OSHA aligned the standard with the Globally Harmonized System in 2012, the old free-form MSDS has been replaced by the standardized **16-section SDS**: same sections, same order, from every manufacturer.

A Level II uses the SDS as a working tool, not shelf paper. Before a new penetrant, remover, or developer enters the line, read it in this order: **Section 2** for the hazard classification and pictograms; **Section 8** for exposure limits and the specific gloves, eye protection, and ventilation the material demands; **Section 9** for the flash point that sets its storage class; **Section 7** for storage rules and incompatibilities; **Section 4** posted where first aid would actually happen. And when you write or revise a PT procedure — this module's other job — those sections are where the safety paragraphs come from.

sections.caption: The 16-section SDS, mapped to the questions a PT shop actually asks.
rows: Which gloves and what ventilation this remover needs
Exposure limits (PEL/TLV) and the specific PPE the manufacturer prescribes
Whether the new solvent belongs in the flammable cabinet
Flash point, vapor pressure, evaporation rate
What to do for penetrant splashed in the eyes
Route-by-route first aid and the symptoms to expect
How and where to store it
Storage temperature limits, incompatible materials, ignition precautions
GHS classification, pictograms, and health effects by route of exposure
What to do when a drum tips over
Containment, cleanup methods, and PPE for the spill response

figures.title: The flash-point ladder: where PT materials sit
Inside the UV-A lamp: where the protection lives

interactive.title: Close the Four Doors: A Shift on the Fluorescent Line
interactive.intro: You are the Level II running a fluorescent penetrant line with one Level I trainee. Four situations come at you during the shift. Make the call a certificate holder is expected to make.

steps.prompt: A drum of hydrophilic remover arrives from a manufacturer you have not used before. Before it goes anywhere near the tank, where do you look to choose gloves and confirm the ventilation it needs?
options: SDS Section 9 — Physical and chemical properties
The shipping papers that came with the drum
SDS Section 8 — Exposure controls / personal protection
Use the same gloves the previous remover called for
steps.feedback: Section 8 is where the manufacturer states exposure limits and prescribes the specific PPE — glove material, eye protection, ventilation. Section 9 holds physical data like flash point, shipping papers cover transport, and glove choice is material-specific: the last product's glove may not resist this one.

steps.prompt: Mid-shift, you notice a hairline crack across the deep-violet filter of the booth's mercury-vapor UV lamp. The rack in process is half inspected. What do you do?
options: Finish the rack under it, then swap the lamp at the break
Cover the crack with clear tape and log it for maintenance
Keep working — UV-A is the least energetic ultraviolet band
Take the lamp out of service immediately and finish under a serviceable lamp
steps.feedback: The filter is the only thing converting the bulb's broadband output — which includes UV-B and UV-C — into a safe UV-A beam. A cracked, loose, or missing filter can leak the wavelengths that burn eyes and skin, so the lamp comes out of service immediately. Tape is not a filter, and 'UV-A is mild' does not apply to what leaks past a broken one.

steps.prompt: Your trainee finishes unloading the penetrant tank, grabs the solvent-remover can, and starts wiping dye off his bare forearms with a soaked rag. Your move?
options: Let it go — the solvent is formulated to remove exactly this penetrant
Stop him: skin gets soap and water, and gloves and sleeves keep dye off it in the first place
Allow it as long as the solvent is not chlorinated
Have him do it under the exhaust hood so vapors are controlled
steps.feedback: Solvent strips the skin's protective oils along with the dye — repeated 'solvent washing' is a classic route to dermatitis. The fix is soap and water now, plus the gloves and dry cuffs that should have prevented the contact. Chlorinated or not, and hood or not, solvent on skin is the hazard.

steps.prompt: Doing the end-of-shift walkdown, you find a full case of aerosol nonaqueous developer stored on top of the drying oven, where it is distinctly warm to the touch. What is the right disposition?
options: Move the case to the approved flammable-liquids cabinet, away from heat sources
Leave it — sealed cans are safe until they are sprayed
Vent each can slightly to relieve the pressure, then leave the case where it is
It is acceptable as long as the oven stays at its normal operating setting
steps.feedback: Aerosol developer is a flammable solvent in a pressurized can; labels typically limit storage to 120 °F (49 °C), and a dryer top can exceed that. Heated cans can rupture and feed a fire. They go in the flammable-liquids cabinet — and you never puncture or 'vent' a pressurized can.

checkQuestions.question: What exactly is a liquid's flash point?
options: The temperature at which the liquid boils and converts entirely to vapor
The temperature at which the liquid ignites spontaneously without any spark or flame
The lowest temperature at which the liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with the air at its surface
The maximum temperature at which the liquid can be stored in a sealed container
checkQuestions.explanation: Flash point is the lowest temperature at which a liquid produces an ignitable vapor-air mixture at its surface — an ignition source is still required. Self-ignition without a spark is the autoignition temperature, a different and much higher number, and flash point is not a boiling point or a container rating.

checkQuestions.question: What is the safety reason a mercury-vapor UV lamp with a cracked filter must come out of service immediately?
options: The bulb's raw output includes hazardous UV-B and UV-C, and the filter is what blocks them from reaching eyes and skin
A cracked filter scatters the beam and slows the inspection down
The filter contains the mercury vapor, which escapes through any crack
Cracks let white light into the booth and ruin the inspectors' dark adaptation
checkQuestions.explanation: The mercury arc emits broadband ultraviolet, including the UV-B and UV-C wavelengths responsible for photokeratitis and skin burns. The deep-violet filter passes only the 320–400 nm UV-A band, so a cracked or missing filter can leak hazardous wavelengths. Mercury is contained in the bulb envelope, not the filter, and beam quality or stray light are process issues, not the safety reason.

checkQuestions.question: A new solvent remover arrives and you must decide whether it belongs in the flammable-liquids cabinet. Which SDS section gives you the flash point?
options: Section 2 — Hazard identification
Section 4 — First-aid measures
Section 14 — Transport information
Section 9 — Physical and chemical properties
checkQuestions.explanation: Flash point is a physical property and is listed in Section 9 along with vapor pressure and evaporation rate. Section 2 states the GHS hazard classification that results from that flash point, but the number itself — what you need to assign a storage class — lives in Section 9.

checkQuestions.question: A technician must hand-wipe solvent remover on weld surfaces inside a closed vessel. What is the principal inhalation concern?
options: Solvent vapor enriches the oxygen level inside the vessel and raises the fire risk
Solvent vapors accumulate in the confined space and can reach harmful concentrations or displace breathable air
Dry developer dust will be stirred up by the wiping action
Ultraviolet light inside the vessel generates ozone
checkQuestions.explanation: In a confined space, solvent vapor has nowhere to go: concentrations climb toward harmful levels and the vapor can displace breathable air. That is why vessel entry is governed by a confined-space program with ventilation, monitoring, and respiratory protection — never improvised. Solvent vapor displaces oxygen rather than enriching it, no developer is in use, and ozone from UV-A is not the driving hazard.

checkQuestions.question: Why is an open dip tank of AMS 2644-qualified penetrant a relatively low fire hazard at normal shop temperature?
options: Penetrants are water-based, so they cannot burn
The fluorescent dye acts as a flame suppressant
Qualified penetrants must have high flash points — no lower than 93 °C (200 °F) — so at shop temperature the tank gives off no ignitable vapor
The steel walls of the tank conduct heat away faster than a fire can start
checkQuestions.explanation: AMS 2644 requires qualified penetrants to flash at or above 93 °C (200 °F), placing them in NFPA 30 Class IIIB — far above room temperature, so the open tank does not produce an ignitable vapor blanket. Penetrants are oil-based, dye has no suppressant effect, and the real flammables on a PT line are the Class I solvent removers and nonaqueous developer carriers.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §5.0 (safety and health: toxicity, flammability, ultraviolet precautions, SDS)
OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200, Hazard Communication (GHS container labels and the 16-section safety data sheet)
SAE AMS 2644, Inspection Material, Penetrant (material qualification, including minimum flash point)
NFPA 30, Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code (flash-point classes and storage requirements)
